manifest.title: Engine Alert Spam Incident
manifest.description: Diagnosis, remediation, and post-deployment verification for the July 11–12 engine validation alert incident.

cards.description: Last observed loop before the performance fix.
First complete loop after deploying the allocation-history cache.
Second complete loop after deploying the allocation-history cache.

charts.title: Hourly RiskGateway reject rate
charts.subtitle: Rolling deployment evidence, July 11–12; last hour is partial
charts.question: Was the absolute reject count a genuine rate spike?
charts.rationale: An hourly line preserves the numerator and denominator context across enough periods to distinguish volume from degradation.

comparisonContext.denominator: All RiskGateway decisions in each hour

charts.title: Repeated reject-alert count
charts.subtitle: Twelve notifications arrived at five-minute intervals on July 11
charts.question: Was Discord receiving distinct incidents or repeated copies of one condition?
charts.rationale: The ordered alert series makes the fixed five-minute cadence and slowly changing detail explicit.

comparisonContext.denominator: Rejects in the configured six-hour digest window

charts.title: Engine loop duration before and after the fix
charts.subtitle: Complete observed loops; 60-second loop interval shown as the reference
charts.question: Did the allocation-history cache restore the engine loop budget?
charts.rationale: A horizontal bar comparison makes the three discrete observed durations and the 60-second operating threshold directly comparable.

query.sql: SELECT date_trunc('hour', to_timestamp(created_at_ms / 1000.0) AT TIME ZONE 'Europe/Amsterdam') AS hour_local, count(*) FILTER (WHERE decision = 'reject') AS rejects, count(*) AS decisions, round(100.0 * count(*) FILTER (WHERE decision = 'reject') / nullif(count(*), 0), 2) AS reject_pct FROM risk_gateway_decisions WHERE created_at_ms >= extract(epoch FROM now() - interval '24 hours') * 1000 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;
query.description: Hourly reject numerator, total-decision denominator, and reject percentage for the 24 hours ending at the incident verification snapshot.
metric_definitions: Reject rate = reject decisions divided by all RiskGateway decisions in the same hour, multiplied by 100.

query.sql: SELECT violation->>'code' AS violation, count(*) AS count FROM risk_gateway_decisions CROSS JOIN LATERAL jsonb_array_elements(violations_json::jsonb) violation WHERE decision = 'reject' AND created_at_ms >= extract(epoch FROM now() - interval '1 hour') * 1000 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC;
query.description: Counts violation codes attached to rejected RiskGateway decisions in the latest one-hour diagnostic window.
metric_definitions: Violation count is the number of reject decisions carrying each deterministic RiskGateway violation code.

query.sql: SELECT service_role, instance_id, started_at_ms, updated_at_ms, metadata_json #>> '{engine_loop,service,last_run_duration_ms}' AS last_run_duration_ms, metadata_json #> '{engine_loop,service,last_stage_ms}' AS last_stage_ms FROM service_heartbeats WHERE service_role = 'trader' ORDER BY updated_at_ms DESC;
query.query: Read the runtime status endpoint and select the current trader heartbeat plus engine-loop duration fields.
query.description: Pre-fix and post-fix total loop and candidate-pipeline durations captured from the trader heartbeat.
metric_definitions: Total loop duration is wall-clock milliseconds for EngineService.run_once.
Candidate-pipeline duration is accumulated wall-clock milliseconds spent scoring, allocating, risk-checking, reviewing, attributing, and simulating candidates.

query.query: Twelve risk_rejects_spike messages posted between 21:00 and 21:55 Europe/Amsterdam on July 11, 2026.
query.description: The incident excerpt supplied by the operator, transcribed into timestamp and reported-reject-count rows.
metric_definitions: Reported rejects is the absolute reject count printed in each Discord message.

query.description: Source inspection comparing the five-minute time-bucket dedupe and six-hour absolute-count window with the deployed transition-only, rate-aware implementation.
metric_definitions: Old alert dedupe bucket = max(300 seconds, stale-loop threshold).
New alert threshold requires at least 20 rejects, 50 total decisions, and a 20% reject rate in 900 seconds.

query.sql: SELECT category, count(*) FROM operational_notification_outbox WHERE scheduled_at_ms >= 1783849578103 AND source_type = 'engine_validation_monitor' GROUP BY category ORDER BY category;
query.description: Counts validation-monitor notifications enqueued after the corrected trader instance started.
metric_definitions: Alert messages are rows with category engine_validation_alert; the normal six-hour digest uses engine_validation_digest and may span multiple Discord chunks.

blocks.body:
## Executive Summary

- **This was primarily an alert-definition and deduplication regression, not a RiskGateway failure.** The rollout increased the number of shadow evaluations, while the monitor counted rejects across a six-hour window without considering the much larger decision denominator.
- **The Discord repetition was deterministic.** One continuing condition received a fresh dedupe bucket every five minutes, producing twelve near-identical warnings in 55 minutes. Notifications now fire only when an incident opens or escalates.
- **The loop overrun was real and has been corrected.** A repeated allocation-history query inside every candidate evaluation drove the candidate pipeline to 55.0 seconds. One snapshot is now reused within each loop; the first two verified loops completed in 13.8 and 29.1 seconds, below the 60-second interval.

## Absolute reject counts hid a low failure rate

The alert used a threshold of five rejects over the same six-hour window as the validation digest. After Wave 2 increased shadow candidate volume, that threshold became almost certain to fire. Hourly reject rates remained between 0% and 2.63% in the reviewed deployment window; the corrected monitor requires both material volume and a 20% reject rate over 15 minutes.

**Implication:** deterministic RiskGateway rejections still block weak-edge or poor-market-quality candidates, but ordinary safety filtering no longer masquerades as an operational incident.

## The rejects were normal policy outcomes

The latest one-hour diagnostic sample contained 35 rejects versus 1,588 allows. All rejects were explained by two deterministic controls: 20 candidates were below the minimum net edge and 15 encountered spreads above the engine limit.

**Implication:** the gate was doing useful filtering; weakening it would have addressed the symptom by admitting worse candidates.

## A five-minute bucket recycled one incident

The operator-visible sequence was one persistent condition, not twelve separate spikes. Reported counts moved only from 90 to 103 while a new message arrived every five minutes.

**Implication:** the monitor now retains active incident state and posts only on opening or severity escalation. A continuing warning does not generate reminder spam.

## Alert semantics now match incident semantics

The old key included a five-minute time bucket. The deployed key is tied to the incident start and severity, and active state is cleared only after the condition disappears. Reject-spike detection now uses a 15-minute window with count, denominator, and rate gates.

**Implication:** a resolved condition may alert again if it genuinely reopens, while an unresolved condition remains quiet.

## Candidate processing is back inside the loop budget

Before the fix, each candidate independently loaded and scanned up to 5,000 historical allocations. Reusing that read-only snapshot within the loop reduced the candidate-pipeline stage from 55.0 seconds to 4.9 seconds on the first run and 19.7 seconds on the second. Both complete loops stayed below the 60-second watchdog threshold.

**Implication:** strategy breadth remains enabled; the fix removes duplicate database work instead of suppressing evidence generation.

## Post-deployment verification is clean

The first corrected watchdog observed 13 rejects out of 381 decisions, or 3.41%, and opened no active incident. No `engine_validation_alert` row entered the Discord outbox after the corrected trader started; only the normal validation digest was enqueued.

**Implication:** monitoring is active again, without weakening shadow-only execution, RiskGateway, Council review, or readiness blocking.

## Recommended Next Steps

1. Keep the new 15-minute, 20-reject, 50-decision, 20% rate threshold unchanged for one full day.
2. Review the hourly reject-rate distribution after 24 hours and adjust only if normal operation approaches the threshold.
3. Preserve transition-only delivery for all watchdog conditions; add a deliberately long reminder interval only if operators later request one.
4. Continue tracking candidate-pipeline duration separately from total loop duration so future strategy breadth does not hide database regressions.

## Further Questions

- Does the post-fix candidate pipeline remain below 45 seconds at the highest observed candidate volume over the next 24 hours?
- Should market-quality rejections such as wide spreads receive a separate informational digest rather than share an operational incident metric?

## Caveats and Assumptions

- The last hourly reject-rate point is partial.
- The latency comparison contains one pre-fix loop and two immediate post-fix loops; it proves the regression is removed in the observed runs, not a long-run percentile target.
- Incident state is process-local. A trader restart may emit one fresh opening notification for a still-active condition, but it will not repeat every five minutes thereafter.
- Normal six-hour validation digests remain enabled and can span multiple Discord chunks.
